patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a concern about handling ENOMEM errors in btrfs_insert_dir_item() without causing an abort, specifically focusing on whether this approach will lead to further ENOMEM errors in other functions like btrfs_orphan_add(). The patch aims to provide more concrete evidence that this handling strategy is effective and worth the added complexity.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jeff Layton expressed concern that even if ENOMEM is handled gracefully, the patch may still result in an abort due to subsequent errors in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation in that codepath by stashing it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle().
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is
btrfs_alloc_path()? We could consider preallocating that too -- maybe
stash it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle?

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Jeff Layton's concern about the patch handling ENOMEM in btrfs_orphan_add() by explaining that when ENOMEM occurs, it will instead call btrfs_orphan_add(), which is likely to also fail with ENOMEM.

Author expressed confusion about reviewer's concern, stating they had provided inline comments near the relevant code and apologizing for any misunderstanding.

Author acknowledges reviewer's concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item() might not be sufficient, as it could still lead to an abort in btrfs_orphan_add(). Author promises to resend evidence to support the complexity of the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
And when we get ENOMEM, we call btrfs_orphan_add() isntead of
unilaterally aborting. I contend that this call is now likely to ENOMEM

---

I am still a bit confused. I made comments inline closer to the the code.
Sorry if I am being dense or missing the point!

---

Take a peek at https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/cover.1782249000.git.boris@bur.io/

which I need to RESEND :)

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested using on-stack memory for btrfs_alloc_path() to avoid ENOMEM issues, citing the practice used in btrfs-progs and the small size of the structure (112 bytes), but did not recommend making this change universally for all btrfs_path instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
For btrfs_alloc_path(), you can just use on-stack memory for that.

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a concern about handling ENOMEM errors in btrfs_insert_dir_item() without causing an abort, specifically focusing on whether this approach will lead to further ENOMEM errors in other functions like btrfs_orphan_add(). The patch aims to provide more concrete evidence that this handling strategy is effective and worth the added complexity.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jeff Layton expressed concerns about the practicality of reproducing the ENOMEM issue in btrfs_insert_dir_item() under heavy memory pressure, but noted that there are records of filesystem aborts where this patch would have prevented them. He suggested using ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() to make testing easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
I didn't really try to reproduce this in earnest. We only see it in our
fleet under heavy memory pressure, and even then at such low frequency,
I doubt our chances of hitting this on anything other than a huge set
of machines.

So, theoretical / structural, but we have record of filesystem aborts
where the stack indicates that this would have prevented it. Userland
would have gotten an -ENOMEM back but the fs wouldn't have aborted.

I see that there are some ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls in btrfs. We
could wire some of these functions up with that, which would make this
easier to test. I'll look into that in the meantime.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() does not necessarily prevent aborts in btrfs_orphan_add(), and requests more concrete evidence of the patch's benefits.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in
btrfs_orphan_add() and abort anyway. That is why I was wanting to see
some more concrete evidence this actually helps to make it worth the
complexity.

Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: Add missing sctx check in cleanup path

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: handle -ENOMEM errors in some synchronous dirops without aborting

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: handle -ENOMEM errors in some synchronous dirops without aborting

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] btrfs: split btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index() into prealloc and commit phases
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the `btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index()` function into three separate functions: `btrfs_prealloc_delayed_dir_index()`, `btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index_prealloc()`, and `btrfs_free_delayed_dir_index_prealloc()`. The new functions are designed to handle memory allocation and btree insertion separately, allowing for cleaner error handling and reducing the risk of aborting transactions due to out-of-memory conditions. The original function is refactored into a thin wrapper that calls these new functions. This change enables callers to move fallible memory allocations before the point of no return, making it easier to handle errors without aborting transactions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] btrfs: pre-allocate delayed dir index before btree modification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_insert_dir_item function to pre-allocate delayed dir index resources before modifying the btree. This change prevents ENOMEM failures from leaving the btree in a partially-modified state, and allows for more graceful error handling. The patch also introduces an optional parameter to allow callers to provide their own pre-allocated resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to handle ENOMEM errors more gracefully when inserting directory items. Instead of aborting transactions under memory pressure, it converts newly-created inodes into orphans and returns -ENOMEM to userspace. This change affects create(), mkdir(), mknod(), symlink(), and link() operations. The approach taken involves modifying the btrfs_add_link() function to handle ENOMEM errors and adding orphan handling in btrfs_create_new_inode().

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov questioned whether it's possible for delayed dir index pre-allocation to fail, but btrfs_orphan_add() to succeed due to its own memory allocations, and asked if the issue has been reproduced in practice.
review_comments.raw_body:
I feel like the crux of this series to me is whether you have practical
conditions where the allocation of the delayed_node is failing, but the
allocations involved in btrfs_orphan_add() succeed. It allocates a
btrfs_path and has to walk the btree which might have to read the node
at every level which might need to allocate 16k extent buffers and
extent buffer objects and xarray storage for each one. For size
reference, on my build (maybe debug..?) a delayed_node is 552 bytes,
while a btrfs_path is 112 and an extent_buffer is 432. So they are
pretty similar in size (not to mention the 16k of node file backed
memory we are sort of likely to have to allocate if we are under
reclaim)

Were you able to reproduce this issue and help in practice or is this a
theoretical / structural improvement?

With that said, all the prealloc wiring looks good to me in general, and
it seems to be a pretty clean win for the "name exists" case in the next
patch.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch's effectiveness in preventing filesystem aborts under heavy memory pressure, acknowledging it's difficult to reproduce but noting there are existing records of such incidents and proposing to use ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls to make testing easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
I didn't really try to reproduce this in earnest. We only see it in our
fleet under heavy memory pressure, and even then at such low frequency,
I doubt our chances of hitting this on anything other than a huge set
of machines.

So, theoretical / structural, but we have record of filesystem aborts
where the stack indicates that this would have prevented it. Userland
would have gotten an -ENOMEM back but the fs wouldn't have aborted.

I see that there are some ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls in btrfs. We
could wire some of these functions up with that, which would make this
easier to test. I'll look into that in the meantime.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() may not be effective, as it could still lead to an abort when calling btrfs_orphan_add(), and requested concrete evidence of its benefits.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in
btrfs_orphan_add() and abort anyway. That is why I was wanting to see
some more concrete evidence this actually helps to make it worth the
complexity.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential issue where objects are allocated successfully but then fail to be added as orphans due to an error in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation (btrfs_alloc_path?) in the transaction handle.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is
btrfs_alloc_path()? We could consider preallocating that too -- maybe
stash it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle?

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov noted that when handling ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item(), the patch calls btrfs_orphan_add() which itself may fail and return ENOMEM, potentially leading to an infinite loop of errors.

Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed confusion about the patch's handling of ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item(), specifically asking for clarification on the inline comments he made earlier.

Reviewer pointed out that the patch description and code changes do not match, as the patch description mentions delayed dir index pre-allocation but the code does not implement it.
review_comments.raw_body:
And when we get ENOMEM, we call btrfs_orphan_add() isntead of
unilaterally aborting. I contend that this call is now likely to ENOMEM

---

I am still a bit confused. I made comments inline closer to the the code.
Sorry if I am being dense or missing the point!

---

Take a peek at https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/cover.1782249000.git.boris@bur.io/

which I need to RESEND :)

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested using on-stack memory for btrfs_alloc_path() to avoid potential trans aborts due to ENOMEM, citing a similar practice in btrfs-progs and the relatively small size of the structure (112 bytes).
review_comments.raw_body:
For btrfs_alloc_path(), you can just use on-stack memory for that.

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] btrfs: pre-allocate delayed dir index for non-overwrite rename
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a feature to the Btrfs file system that pre-allocates delayed directory index resources before performing non-overwrite renames. This ensures that ENOMEM errors are returned early, preventing potential data corruption. The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_prealloc_delayed_dir_index` and modifies existing functions such as `btrfs_add_link` to handle pre-allocated resources. The goal is to improve the robustness of Btrfs by catching memory allocation failures before making irreversible changes to the file system's internal state.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v7 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 1/6] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) and per-procedure call counts to the svc_stat structure in the sunrpc module. The existing global counters are not network-namespace-aware, which is a problem for the upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics. To solve this issue, the patch introduces per-netns equivalents of the global counters and provides helper functions to manage them. The changes include adding a pointer array to struct svc_stat, incrementing the per-net counter alongside the global one in svc_generic_init_request(), and calling the alloc/free helpers from nfsd_net_init() and nfsd_net_exit().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 2/6] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace arrays instead of a global array. This change allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts on a per-network-namespace basis, which is useful for monitoring and debugging purposes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 3/6] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements a new netlink handler for the NFS server, allowing users to retrieve server statistics via netlink. The handler streams statistics across multiple messages, dividing them into sections such as scalar stats and per-version procedure counts. This approach prevents overflow issues when packing counters into a single message. The patch also includes documentation updates and code changes in various files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 4/6] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters across various NFS-related modules. The removal is justified by the fact that svc_seq_show() and nfsd netlink stats handler no longer read these global counters. This change simplifies the code and reduces unnecessary memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 5/6] nfsd: count NFSv4 callback operations per netns
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-netns counters to track NFSv4 callback operations in the Linux kernel's nfsd module. It introduces a new array of percpu counters for callback operations, indexed by RFC 8881 callback opcode, and updates the relevant counter in nfsd4_run_cb(). The patch also removes redundant counters and their associated helpers, improving statistics collection and accuracy.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 6/6] nfsd: export NFSv4 callback op stats via netlink
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to export NFSv4 callback operation statistics via netlink. It introduces a new attribute called 'proc4cb-ops' which is nested under the existing 'server-stats' netlink dump. This allows for the reporting of NFSv4 backchannel operation counts, including CB_GETATTR, over netlink. The patch reuses the existing server-proc-entry (op/count) layout and adds a callback section that emits one entry per callback opcode from the per-netns callback counters. This enables nfsstat to report NFSv4 backchannel operation counts over netlink.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink, addressing limitations in the current procfs interface such as global counters and fragile parsing format. It adds per-network-namespace procedure call counts to the sunrpc layer and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Applied the patches to nfsd-testing without any issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 17 Jul 2026 07:08:52 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> I last sent this series about a month ago, and dropped the ball on
> sending out a newer version afterward. This version fixes some minor
> issues that Chuck and Sashiko pointed out in review.
> 
> The nfsstat tool currently scrapes /proc/net/rpc/nfsd for server
> statistics. This procfs interface has several limitations: the
> counters are global (not network-namespace-aware), the format is
> fragile to parse, and it cannot be extended without breaking
> existing parsers.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[2/6] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
      commit: 07d348fc22493ddf7f195903b929ddae1dd0f533
[3/6] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
      commit: 58656de1ad40362db64f5ac6debd2646b956fe1a
[4/6] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
      commit: 09048531826cc3a5b0baca029ce3e3af432029aa
[5/6] nfsd: count NFSv4 callback operations per netns
      commit: 5ce4d8f4b06b8f987b1cc88f13e8c1a1cf29db37
[6/6] nfsd: export NFSv4 callback op stats via netlink
      commit: 134cf2c7e0bc44e69d52d955bac1c2348a86832f

--
Chuck Lever

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a concern about handling ENOMEM errors in btrfs_insert_dir_item() without causing an abort, specifically focusing on whether this approach will lead to further ENOMEM errors in other functions like btrfs_orphan_add(). The patch aims to provide more concrete evidence that this handling strategy is effective and worth the added complexity.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() does not necessarily prevent aborts in btrfs_orphan_add(), and requests more concrete evidence of the patch's benefits.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in
btrfs_orphan_add() and abort anyway. That is why I was wanting to see
some more concrete evidence this actually helps to make it worth the
complexity.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jeff Layton expressed concern that even if ENOMEM is handled gracefully, the patch may still result in an abort due to subsequent errors in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation in that codepath by stashing it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle().
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is
btrfs_alloc_path()? We could consider preallocating that too -- maybe
stash it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle?

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Jeff Layton's concern about the patch handling ENOMEM in btrfs_orphan_add() by explaining that when ENOMEM occurs, it will instead call btrfs_orphan_add(), which is likely to also fail with ENOMEM.

Author expressed confusion about reviewer's concern, stating they had provided inline comments near the relevant code and apologizing for any misunderstanding.

Author acknowledges reviewer's concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item() might not be sufficient, as it could still lead to an abort in btrfs_orphan_add(). Author promises to resend evidence to support the complexity of the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
And when we get ENOMEM, we call btrfs_orphan_add() isntead of
unilaterally aborting. I contend that this call is now likely to ENOMEM

---

I am still a bit confused. I made comments inline closer to the the code.
Sorry if I am being dense or missing the point!

---

Take a peek at https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/cover.1782249000.git.boris@bur.io/

which I need to RESEND :)

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested using on-stack memory for btrfs_alloc_path() to avoid ENOMEM issues, citing the practice used in btrfs-progs and the small size of the structure (112 bytes), but did not recommend making this change universally for all btrfs_path instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
For btrfs_alloc_path(), you can just use on-stack memory for that.

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/5] NFSD: Eliminate percpu counter contention in DRC memory accounting

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/5] Minor NFSD global DRC clean-ups

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/3] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init
[PATCH v2 0/3] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init
[PATCH v3 0/3] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] fuse: fix missing barrier when checking io-uring readiness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a missing barrier in the FUSE kernel module, which can lead to a CPU observing that io-uring initialization is complete before it actually is. This can cause a deadlock that was previously fixed by commit 3393ff964e0f. The patch adds an smp_rmb() barrier to pair with the existing smp_wmb() in fuse_chan_set_initialized(), ensuring that the two loads are properly ordered and preventing the deadlock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/3] fuse: use release/acquire for fch->initialized
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code where lockless readers may access the 'initialized' flag of a connection before it has been properly set by the writer, leading to undefined behavior. To fix this, the patch introduces release/acquire semantics for writing and reading the 'initialized' flag using smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire(). This ensures that writers and readers are properly synchronized, preventing potential issues. The changes are made in three files: cuse.c, dev.c, and dev_uring.c. The patch is not marked as stable because it addresses a rare edge case that should not occur on well-behaved servers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] fuse: publish io-uring queues with release semantics
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential race condition in the io-uring implementation of the FUSE file system by ensuring that the initialization of fuse_ring_queue structures is properly ordered before their publication. It achieves this by replacing WRITE_ONCE() with smp_store_release() to guarantee that the queue's field initialization happens before its pointer assignment. Additionally, it uses READ_ONCE() for readers accessing the queues to prevent undefined behavior in case of concurrent access.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses missing barriers in the io-uring initialization of the fuse filesystem, fixing issues reported by Sashiko. The patches introduce release/acquire semantics for fch->initialized and publish io-uring queues with release semantics.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Identified and fixed a missing barrier in io-uring initialization. Approved the patch series for application.

On Thu, 16 Jul 2026 at 20:31, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the creation of fuse_ring from entry registration in FUSE connections. The ring is now created during initialization after a server has negotiated and set FUSE_OVER_IO_URING, rather than being lazily created on the first FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command. This change eliminates concurrent creation issues and simplifies code by removing open-coded handling in fuse_uring_create(). The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this decoupling.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi questioned whether fuse_uring_register() should block if the fuse ring is not yet set up, indicating a need for further consideration.
review_comments.raw_body:
What about if the fuse ring is not yet set up?  Should
fuse_uring_register() wait in that case?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author clarified that the fuse ring is created when processing FUSE_INIT reply, and userspace will get -EAGAIN if it issues a REGISTER before the ring is published.
review_comments.raw_body:
The fuse ring is set up when the kernel processes the FUSE_INIT reply.
If userspace races ahead and issues a REGISTER before the FUSE_INIT
reply is processed, they get back -EAGAIN and will need to retry,
which is the same as the pre-existing behavior. The ring gets
published before fch->initialized gets set.

Not sure if this answers your question or if I'm misreading what you're asking.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi questioned whether retrying the INIT reply in a loop after completing the write() operation would be good behavior, and suggested that the REGISTER op should block until initialization is complete.
review_comments.raw_body:
That was my question, yes.

Retrying in a loop doesn't sound like good behavior.

I was thinking that with sync INIT the completing the write() with the
INIT reply does not mean that the connection gets initialized.

Shouldn't the REGISTER op block until initialization is complete?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a pre-existing issue in the sync case and plans to address it in a separate patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, this would be more ideal for the sync case. libfuse currently
works around this with the retry loop.

This is a pre-existing issue that's not introduced by this series
though, so I'll send a patch out for this separately.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/6] fuse: add FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE command to the fuse-over-io-uring implementation, allowing servers to create queues explicitly and decoupling queue setup from entry registration. This is necessary for the subsequent addition of buffer pools to existing queues. The patch modifies the fuse_uring_create_queue function to take an additional flag indicating whether to fail if the queue already exists, and updates the fuse_uring_register function to use this new command when creating a queue lazily. The changes also include updates to error handling and logging in various places.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 3/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation over io-uring, allowing the server to pass in a buffer pool that the kernel can use for servicing requests. This decouples the relationship between entries and payload buffers, enabling the kernel to optimize memory usage and reduce requirements for using fuse-over-io-uring. The patch adds support for registering a buffer pool per queue with the FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL command, which is used by the server to pass in a contiguous chunk of memory. The kernel then splits this region into buffers based on the maximum payload size and tracks free buffers with a bitmap. On dispatch, requests that need a payload claim a free buffer, and on completion, the buffer is returned to the pool or reused directly if needed again.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 4/6] fuse: support registered buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use a buffer pool that is also registered through io-uring. This eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request. The server can register a buffer pool by setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFPOOL flag when adding a bufpool through the FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL cmd. The patch includes benchmark results showing improved write performance with registered buffers, bringing it closer to read performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 5/6] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. The server can specify which open files should use this feature by setting a flag during opens. This requires administrative privileges and is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN. The patch eliminates unnecessary memory copies between the application, kernel, and server for read/write operations, improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 6/6] docs: fuse: document io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy uapi
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) subsystem, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy functionality. The new documentation explains how buffer pools decouple entries from payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory management and reducing overhead. Additionally, it describes zero-copy, a feature that enables direct I/O operations between the server and client's pages without an intermediary copy, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. This patch aims to improve understanding of FUSE over io-uring usage and its capabilities.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools to the FUSE file system, allowing servers to pass in a contiguous chunk of memory that the kernel can use to service requests. This decouples the request and payload buffers, reducing memory usage requirements and enabling optimizations for assigning payload memory. Additionally, the series introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows servers with CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The zero-copy feature requires using buffer pools and has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec changes. The patch series includes benchmark results showing significant throughput improvements for direct-I/O operations, particularly when backing I/O is not the bottleneck.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to decouple fuse_ring creation from entity registration, but the discussion reveals a deeper issue of synchronization between userspace and kernel initialization.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential synchronization issue between userspace and kernel initialization, suggesting that the REGISTER operation should block until initialization is complete.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 17 Jul 2026 at 18:28, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Fri, Jul 17, 2026 at 2:32 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >
> > On Thu, 16 Jul 2026 at 19:59, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> > >
> > > Currently, the connection's fuse_ring is created lazily on the first
> > > FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command. A server registers entries from one
> > > thread per queue (one per CPU) and those threads issue their first
> > > REGISTER command concurrently. They then race to create the single
> > > per-connection fuse_ring, which required open-coded handling in
> > > fuse_uring_create() to detect and protect against concurrent creations.
> > >
> > > Decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration and move it to
> > > FUSE_INIT reply processing after a server has negotiated and set
> > > FUSE_OVER_IO_URING. The ring is published before the connection is
> > > marked initialized. fuse_uring_register() no longer creates the ring and
> > > it instead uses the ring set up at init time.
> >
> > What about if the fuse ring is not yet set up?  Should
> > fuse_uring_register() wait in that case?
>
> The fuse ring is set up when the kernel processes the FUSE_INIT reply.
> If userspace races ahead and issues a REGISTER before the FUSE_INIT
> reply is processed, they get back -EAGAIN and will need to retry,
> which is the same as the pre-existing behavior. The ring gets
> published before fch->initialized gets set.
>
> Not sure if this answers your question or if I'm misreading what you're asking.

That was my question, yes.

Retrying in a loop doesn't sound like good behavior.

I was thinking that with sync INIT the completing the write() with the
INIT reply does not mean that the connection gets initialized.

Shouldn't the REGISTER op block until initialization is complete?

Thanks,
Miklos

> That was my question, yes.

> Retrying in a loop doesn't sound like good behavior.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing after-split folios

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing after-split folios

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch addresses a bug in the huge_memory module where the i_mmap_rwsem is unlocked after releasing after-split folios, leading to a potential use-after-free error. The fix involves moving the unlock of i_mmap_rwsem before the loop that unlocks and frees the after-split folios. This ensures that no inode or mapping dereferences occur once the after-split folios start being unlocked.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Added the patch as a hotfix without further comment or analysis.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 16 Jul 2026 10:54:24 +0100 Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

> From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
> 
> __folio_split() keeps dereferencing the mapping after the split:
> shmem_uncharge(mapping->host) and remap_page() while the folios are still
> frozen/locked, and i_mmap_unlock_read(mapping) at the very end, after the
> after-split folios have been unlocked and freed.
> 
> Nothing holds an inode reference across that. The split relies on @folio
> -- which the beyond-EOF drop loop never removes, as it starts at
> folio_next(folio) -- staying locked and in the page cache to hold off
> eviction. But the unlock loop unlocks @folio before i_mmap_unlock_read()
> runs. If the caller's @lock_at is a tail beyond EOF, as memory_failure()
> passes when splitting a poisoned tail of a shmem THP that reaches past
> i_size during truncation, it too is gone from the page cache; so once
> @folio is unlocked no locked, in-cache folio pins the inode, and a
> concurrent final iput() can evict and RCU-free it before
> i_mmap_unlock_read() touches i_mmap_rwsem:
> 
>   BUG: KASAN: slab-use-after-free in __up_read+0x634/0x790
>    i_mmap_unlock_read include/linux/fs.h:537 [inline]
>    __folio_split+0x732/0x1640 mm/huge_memory.c:4100
>    try_to_split_thp_page+0xab/0x390 mm/memory-failure.c:1675
>    memory_failure+0x1394/0x26e0 mm/memory-failure.c:2470

Added as a hotfix. thanks.

>  mm/huge_memory.c | 12 ++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 12 insertions(+)

Sashiko might have found a pre-existing bug in there:

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260716095424.471052-1-kirill@shutemov.name

(why is mapping_set_update() a) a macro and b) undocumented?)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] mm/zswap: Support batch writeback in shrink_memcg()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch introduces support for batch writeback in shrink_memcg() to improve performance.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the global shrinker being awakened too late, potentially causing performance issues. Suggested implementing proactive reclaimers to mitigate this problem.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Proposed an alternative approach to handle the zswap writeback, suggesting that reclaim should wait for zswap writeback and retry instead of skipping it. Also mentioned a potential solution for the memcg limit case.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 17, 2026 at 9:50 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Thu, Jul 16, 2026 at 1:35 PM Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org> wrote:
> >
> >
> > It is a bit alarming that the store rejection rate is still 43% with
> > batching, but we can worry about this later :)
>
> Yeah I think it's unavoidable at the time the global shrinker is
> awaken, unfortunately :( We need to kick off shrinking way ahead of
> time if we want to avoid this, especially since the rate of zswap out
> is going to be faster than the rate of writeback (CPU vs IO).
>
> Sort of why we implement the proactive reclaimers :)

Arguably, reclaim should wait for zswap writeback and retry, similar
to how allocators wakeup kswapd and retry, instead of just skipping
zswap. I was actually thinking about doing this at least for the memcg
limit case, eliminate the sync shrink_memcg(1) call in zswap_store()
and instead wakeup an async flusher and sleep.

A problem for another day I guess :)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/swap: ghost swapfile with backend switching via Redirect entries

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] mm/zswap: Support batch writeback in shrink_memcg()

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/6] mm: access remote process memory under the per-VMA lock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/6] mm: access remote process memory under the per-VMA lock

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 1/6] x86/mm: add untagged_addr_remote_unlocked()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called untagged_addr_remote_unlocked() to the x86/mm module. The function is used to look up the VMA of an address without taking the mmap lock, which can be contended by other tasks on large machines. This is done by annotating access to mm->context.untag_mask with READ_ONCE() and WRITE_ONCE() to make existing lockless reads explicit and KCSAN-clean. The patch also updates several functions in the arch/x86/include/asm/mmu_context.h, arch/x86/include/asm/uaccess_64.h, and include/linux/uaccess.h files to use the new function or update their behavior accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 2/6] riscv/mm: add untagged_addr_remote_unlocked()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for untagged addresses in the RISC-V architecture, specifically addressing a bug where __access_remote_vm() would untag the remote address using the wrong mask due to reading current->mm instead of the target mm. The patch introduces a new function, untagged_addr_remote_unlocked(), which correctly handles pointer masking and annotates accesses to context.pmlen with READ_ONCE() and WRITE_ONCE() for KCSAN cleanliness.

review_comments.author: Rik van Riel
review_comments.summary: This patch series addresses a bug in __access_remote_vm() where the wrong mask is used for pointer masking due to reading current->mm instead of the target mm. The new function, untagged_addr_remote_unlocked(), correctly handles this and annotates accesses to context.pmlen with READ_ONCE() and WRITE_ONCE().

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 3/6] mm: rename get_user_page_vma_remote() to get_user_page_lookup_vma()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function get_user_page_vma_remote() to get_user_page_lookup_vma(). The renamed function still faults in a page at a given address and looks up the VMA that covers it, but its name now more accurately reflects its purpose. This change is part of a series of patches aimed at cleaning up the namespace for adding a variant of this function where the caller already has the VMA. The patch updates several files to use the new function name, including arch-specific code and mm/memory.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 4/6] mm/gup: add get_user_page_vma() to fault in a page under a held lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called get_user_page_vma() to the Linux kernel's mm/gup.c file. This function is used to fault in a single page under a held lock, either the mmap lock or the per-VMA lock. It simplifies the __get_user_pages() function and allows for more flexible locking behavior. The patch also includes changes to handle specific cases such as VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP VMA and userfaultfd VMA. The goal of this patch is to improve the handling of page faults in the kernel, allowing for more efficient and reliable memory access.

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 5/6] mm: use per-VMA lock in __access_remote_vm() for single-VMA accesses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the __access_remote_vm() function by using a per-VMA lock instead of the global mmap_read_lock() when accessing memory within a single VMA. This reduces contention on large machines where tasks are reading /proc/PID/cmdline, /proc/PID/environ, or /proc/PID/mem. The patch also introduces a new access_remote_vma_ops() helper to handle special cases such as VM_IO/VM_PFNMAP VMAs and COWed pages. The approach taken is to use the per-VMA lock when possible, falling back to the global mmap_lock in case of VMA boundary crossings or other exceptions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH RFC v3 6/6] selftests/mm: cover /proc/pid/mem access to VM_PFNMAP memory
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two tests to the selftests/mm/pfnmap.c file, which cover reading VM_PFNMAP memory through /proc/pid/mem. The first test, procmem_cow_read, maps a private and writable file, writes to a copy-on-write (COW) page, and then reads it back through /proc/self/mem. This exercise the __access_remote_vm() function two ways: directly returning a struct page for COWed pages, and routing to vma->vm_ops->access() for raw PFNs. The second test, procmem_pfn_read, reads a raw Physical Frame Number (PFN) back through ->access(), which is only possible for genuine device memory. These tests ensure that the kernel's handling of VM_PFNMAP memory is correct and consistent.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series improves the way Linux accesses remote process memory by introducing a per-VMA lock in __access_remote_vm(). The per-VMA lock is used when accessing memory within a single VMA, and falls back to the mmap lock when crossing VMA boundaries or encountering errors. A new gup helper, get_user_page_vma(), is introduced to handle page faults and returns pages with the caller's lock held. This change aims to reduce contention on the mmap lock in large multi-threaded applications, improving system monitoring and logging capabilities.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH + QUESTION] bpf: use cond_resched_tasks_rcu_qs in bpf_fd_array_map_clear() loop
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to use cond_resched_tasks_rcu_qs in bpf_fd_array_map_clear() loop, but the approach is questioned by Rik van Riel and Paul E. McKenney.

review_comments.author: Rik van Riel
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach, suggesting it may not be the right solution to the problem.

review_comments.author: Paul E. McKenney
review_comments.summary: Proposed an alternative solution, suggesting the use of cond_resched_tasks_rcu_qs() as a way to avoid holding mutexes across synchronize_rcu_tasks().
